description: Figure 3 presents a comparative optical microscopy analysis (30 μm scale bar) of microstructural evolution in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel versus martensitic Cr-Ni steel under cyclic testing conditions. Subfigure (a) shows the as-received austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel characterized by equiaxed austenite grains with discrete κ-phase precipitates decorating grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) reveals the same alloy after thermal cycling, where caption states the grain boundary κ-phase precipitates have undergone significant thickening/coarsening during high-temperature aging, consistent with thermal fatigue exposure between 293 K and 673 K. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the martensitic Cr-Ni steel before and after testing respectively; (c) exhibits the characteristic lath martensite structure with distinct crystallographic orientation, while (d) shows complete decomposition of the martensitic solid solution into an equilibrium ferrite-carbide mixture with loss of the original martensitic orientation. These micrographs visually demonstrate the contrasting thermal stability mechanisms: precipitation coarsening in the austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C system versus martensite tempering/decomposition in the Cr-Ni steel, supporting the paper's conclusion regarding microstructural degradation pathways under cyclic elasto-plastic deformation and thermal loading.
